title: Negative Interaction Quench Dynamics of Density-Ordered Dipolar Bosons in a One-Dimensional Optical Lattice
authors: Rhombik Roy, N. D. Chavda, Barnali Chakrabarti, Arnaldo Gammal
abstract: We explore the nonequilibrium dynamics of a density-ordered dipolar Bose gas in a finite one-dimensional optical lattice following a negative interaction quench, using the numerically exact multiconfigurational time-dependent Hartree method for bosons. The interaction sign reversal, effectively driving a crossover from long-range to short-range interactions, generates rich intra- and interwell tunneling dynamics spanning superfluid, Mott-insulating, and fragmented regimes. A striking finding is the robustness of the underlying crystal-state correlations against the quench, despite the strong dynamical response. We identify emergent excitation modes, including local breathing and dipole-like oscillations, via real- and momentum-space observables, and quantify tunneling through site-resolved position variance. One- and two-body Glauber correlation functions further uncover a direct connection between tunneling and correlation dynamics. Moreover, we show that combining interaction quenches with lattice-depth ramping enables controllable dynamical engineering, establishing dipolar lattice systems as a promising platform for nonequilibrium quantum simulation.
summary: This paper numerically explores how dipolar bosons in a 1D optical lattice behave after a sudden negative interaction quench. Using the MCTDHB method, the authors track various correlation and fluctuation observables across regimes spanning superfluid to Mott-insulating states. The key finding is the remarkable robustness of the initial crystal-state correlations despite the system undergoing significant dynamic changes.
main_problem: The study investigates the non-equilibrium dynamics of a density-ordered dipolar Bose gas following a sudden negative interaction quench.
main_result: The system exhibits a robust persistence of underlying crystal-state correlations despite strong dynamical responses, with dynamics characterized by distinct intrawell excitations and suppressed interwell tunneling.
method: The dynamics are simulated using the numerically exact multiconfigurational time-dependent Hartree method for bosons (MCTDHB/MCTDH-X).
model_or_system: The system consists of dipolar bosons confined in a finite one-dimensional optical lattice. The Hamiltonian includes both kinetic, lattice potential, and long-range dipolar interaction terms, which undergo a sign reversal quench.
key_observables: Site-resolved position variance, real-space and momentum-space density fluctuations, one- and two-body Glauber correlation functions, and dynamical fragmentation.
important_parameters: The quench protocol involves varying the dipolar interaction strength ($g_d$) from a strongly interacting crystal state ($g_d=15$) through superfluid ($g_d=0$), Mott-insulator ($g_d=0.1$), and fermionized Mott ($g_d=1$) regimes.
main_assumptions: Hard-wall boundary conditions are assumed, and the analysis relies on the MCTDH-X formulation, which is computationally intensive.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of the four key observables (fragmentation, variance, $\deltaho$, $\delta n$) across different quench protocols, demonstrating the transition from coherent to localized dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper details the theoretical framework (Hamiltonian, quench protocol, MCTDH-X method), presents results analyzing the dynamics across various quench targets, and concludes by summarizing the findings regarding correlation robustness.
why_interesting: This work provides detailed insights into non-equilibrium quantum dynamics in dipolar systems, which are highly relevant for simulating complex quantum phases and understanding correlation persistence after sudden changes in interaction parameters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. One-body density ρ(x) (red curve) for N = 6 bosons in S = 3 lattice sites as a function of the dipolar interaction strength gd, with lattice depth fixed at V0 = 8. The calcu- lations are performed using M = 24 orbitals. (a) Superfluid (SF) phase (gd = 0): the density is delocalized, with a pro- nounced maximum in the central well due to the dominance of kinetic and lattice energies over interactions. (b) Mott- insulator (MI) phase (gd = 0.1): three equal density peaks across the wells indicate uniform distribution of two bosons per site and suppressed inter-well overlap. (c) Fermionized Mott (FMI) phase (gd = 1): the characteristic density dip within each well mimics...

FIG. 2. Two-body density ρ(2)(x, x′) for six bosons in three lattice sites across the four emergent phases. For gd = 0, atoms cluster at the center with additional off-diagonal peaks due to superfluid delocalization. Increasing gd depletes the diagonal contribution ρ(2)(x, x), signaling enhanced correla- tions. At gd = 1.0, a correlation hole develops (fermionized Mott phase), while for gd = 15.0 a complete diagonal suppres- sion indicates crystallization driven by dominant long-range interactions.

FIG. 3. Nonequilibrium dynamics of six dipolar bosons in a triple-well lattice following a negative interaction quench from gd = 15 (crystal state) to gd = 0 (superfluid). (a) Dy- namical fragmentation: time evolution of the natural occupa- tions ni/N illustrating the redistribution of particles among orbitals, (b) Lattice site-resolved position variance σ2(t), (c) Real-space density fluctuations δρ(x, t), (d) Momentum-space fluctuations δn(k, t). Computation is done with M = 24 or- bitals.

FIG. 4. Nonequilibrium dynamics of six dipolar bosons in a triple-well lattice following a negative interaction quench from gd = 15 (crystal state) to gd = 0.1 (MI). (a) Dynamical frag- mentation: time evolution of the natural occupations ni/N illustrating the redistribution of particles among orbitals, (b) Lattice site-resolved position variance σ2(t), (c) Real-space density fluctuations δρ(x, t), (d) Momentum-space fluctua- tions δn(k, t). Computation is done with M = 24 orbitals.

FIG. 5. Nonequilibrium dynamics of six dipolar bosons in a triple-well lattice following a negative interaction quench from gd = 15 (crystal state) to gd = 1.0 (fragmented MI). (a) Dynamical fragmentation: time evolution of the natural occu- pations ni/N illustrating the redistribution of particles among orbitals, (b) Lattice site-resolved position variance σ2(t), (c) Real-space density fluctuations δρ(x, t), (d) Momentum-space fluctuations δn(k, t). Computation is done with M = 24 or- bitals.
